items.stem: Join with 'because': He was tired. He went to bed early.

choices.label: He went to bed early because he was tired.
He was tired because he went to bed early.
He went to bed early, he was tired.

items.rationale: 'because' gives the reason — being tired is the reason he slept early.

items.stem: Join with 'although': It was raining. They played outside.

choices.label: Although they played outside, it was raining.
Although it was raining, they played outside.
It was raining because they played outside.

items.rationale: 'although' shows they played in spite of the rain.

items.stem: Join with 'so': She was hungry. She ate a sandwich.

choices.label: She ate a sandwich so she was hungry.
She was hungry, so she ate a sandwich.
She was hungry although she ate a sandwich.

items.rationale: 'so' shows the result — being hungry led her to eat.

items.stem: Join with 'but': I like tea. My brother prefers coffee.

choices.label: I like tea, but my brother prefers coffee.
I like tea because my brother prefers coffee.
I like tea, my brother prefers coffee.

items.rationale: 'but' shows the contrast between the two tastes.

items.stem: Join with 'who': The boy is my cousin. He won the race.

choices.label: The boy who won the race is my cousin.
The boy is my cousin who won the race.
The boy won the race who is my cousin.

items.rationale: 'who won the race' describes the boy, so it comes right after 'The boy'.

items.stem: Join with 'which': The bag is expensive. It is made of leather.

choices.label: The bag is expensive which is made of leather.
The bag, which is made of leather, is expensive.
The bag which expensive is made of leather.

items.rationale: 'which is made of leather' describes the bag and sits next to it.

items.stem: Join with 'both … and': Siti can swim. Kavitha can swim.

choices.label: Both Siti and Kavitha can swim.
Siti can swim both Kavitha.
Both Siti can swim and Kavitha.

items.rationale: 'Both … and …' joins the two people: Both Siti and Kavitha.

items.stem: Join with 'neither … nor': He did not call. He did not write.

choices.label: He did neither call nor write.
He neither called nor wrote.
He neither call nor write.

items.rationale: Use the past tense after 'neither … nor': neither called nor wrote.

items.stem: Join with 'when': The bell rang. We went home.

choices.label: We went home when the bell rang.
When we went home, the bell rang.
The bell rang so we went home when.

items.rationale: 'when the bell rang' tells the time we went home.

items.stem: Join with 'if': You study hard. You will pass.

choices.label: You will pass if you study hard.
If you will pass, you study hard.
You study hard if you will pass.

items.rationale: 'if you study hard' is the condition for passing.

items.stem: Change to reported speech: Ravi said, "I am tired."

choices.label: Ravi said that he was tired.
Ravi said that I am tired.
Ravi said that he is tired.

items.rationale: In reported speech, 'I am' becomes 'he was'.

items.stem: Change to reported speech: She said, "I will help you."

choices.label: She said that she will help me.
She said that she would help me.
She said that I would help you.

items.rationale: 'will' becomes 'would' and 'you' becomes 'me' in reported speech.

items.stem: Join with 'too … to': The box is very heavy. I cannot lift it.

choices.label: The box is too heavy for me to lift.
The box is very heavy to lift me.
The box is too heavy to lift for me it.

items.rationale: 'too heavy for me to lift' means it is so heavy I cannot lift it.

items.stem: Join with 'so … that': The soup was very hot. We could not drink it.

choices.label: The soup was so hot that we could not drink it.
The soup was very hot so that we drink it.
The soup was hot so we could that not drink it.

items.rationale: 'so hot that we could not drink it' keeps the meaning.

items.stem: Join with 'as soon as': The rain stopped. The children went out.

choices.label: As soon as the rain stopped, the children went out.
As soon as the children went out, the rain stopped.
The rain stopped as soon as went out the children.

items.rationale: The children went out right after the rain stopped.

items.stem: Join with 'while': Mum cooked dinner. Dad read the news.

choices.label: Mum cooked dinner while Dad read the news.
While Mum cooked dinner Dad, read the news.
Mum cooked dinner because Dad read the news.

items.rationale: 'while' shows the two things happened at the same time.

items.stem: Join with 'unless': You hurry. You will miss the bus.

choices.label: You will miss the bus unless you hurry.
Unless you will miss the bus, you hurry.
You hurry unless you will miss the bus.

items.rationale: 'unless you hurry' means 'if you do not hurry'.

items.stem: Join with 'not only … but also': She sings. She dances.

choices.label: She not only sings but also dances.
She sings not only but also dances.
Not only she sings but she also dances.

items.rationale: 'not only … but also …' frames the two actions: not only sings but also dances.

items.stem: Join with 'despite': They were tired. They kept working.

choices.label: Despite being tired, they kept working.
Despite they kept working, they were tired.
They were tired despite because they kept working.

items.rationale: 'Despite being tired' means even though they were tired.

items.stem: Join with 'either … or': We can take the bus. We can take the MRT.

choices.label: We can take either the bus or the MRT.
We can either take the bus the MRT or.
Either we can take the bus and the MRT.

items.rationale: 'either … or …' offers a choice: either the bus or the MRT.

SM-synthesis.iCanStatement: I can join or rewrite sentences without changing the meaning.
SM-synthesis.explanation: In synthesis and transformation, you combine two sentences or rewrite one using a given word, keeping the same meaning. Think about what the joining word shows — reason (because), contrast (although, but), time (when, while), condition (if, unless) — and put the parts in the right order.

workedExamples.note: He ran because he was late — 'because' gives the reason.
Reported: Rahim said that he was busy — 'I am' → 'he was'.
